questions.question: Define environment variables and explain their significance in Unix/Linux.
keyPoints: Environment variables are named values storing system configuration
They define the working environment for users and processes
Control executable search path, home directory, shell type, etc.
Inherited by child processes for process communication

questions.question: Differentiate between shell variables and environment variables with an example.
keyPoints: Shell variables are local to current shell only
Environment variables are exported and available to child processes
Use 'var=value' for shell variable
Use 'export var' to make it environment variable
Example: PATH is environment, myvar=5 is shell variable

questions.question: Explain the purpose of the following environment variables: PATH, HOME, SHELL, USER, PWD.
keyPoints: PATH: Colon-separated directories for command search
HOME: User's home directory path
SHELL: Default login shell path (/bin/bash)
USER/USERNAME: Current logged-in username
PWD: Current working directory path

questions.question: Describe the commands used to display environment variables in Linux.
keyPoints: env: displays all environment variables
printenv: displays environment variables (can specify variable name)
set: displays all variables including shell variables and functions
echo $VARIABLE_NAME: displays specific variable value

questions.question: Explain the export command in Linux with syntax and example.
keyPoints: export marks shell variables for export to child processes
Syntax: export VAR_NAME or export VAR=value
Without export, variable is shell variable only
Example: export PATH=$PATH:/new/dir adds to PATH
Use unset to remove exported variables

questions.question: What is the PATH variable? Explain how it is used when executing commands.
keyPoints: PATH is an environment variable containing directory paths
Directories are separated by colons (:)
When command is typed, shell searches PATH directories in order
First matching executable is run
Example: PATH=/usr/bin:/bin means check /usr/bin then /bin

questions.question: How do you set, display, and unset environment variables in bash shell?
keyPoints: Set: VAR=value or export VAR=value
Display: echo $VAR, printenv VAR, or env
Unset: unset VAR_NAME
Persistent: add to ~/.bashrc or /etc/profile
Temporary for command: env VAR=value command

questions.question: Explain environment variables in detail. Discuss their types, how they are created, modified, and their role in process execution with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Environment variables are named values for system configuration
Two types: Shell variables (local) and Environment variables (exported)
Creation: automatic at login or manually using export/set
Modification: export VAR=value updates existing variable
Role in process execution: inherited by child processes, define paths, user preferences
Examples: PATH, HOME, USER, SHELL, PWD, TERM, LANG
Commands: env, printenv, set, export, unset

questions.question: Discuss in detail the commands used to list and manage environment variables in Unix/Linux with their syntax and examples.
keyPoints: env: lists all environment variables
printenv: displays environment variables, syntax: printenv [VAR]
set: displays all variables including shell vars, syntax: set
export: creates environment variables, syntax: export [VAR][=value]
unset: removes variables, syntax: unset VAR_NAME
env VAR=value command: runs command with modified environment
env -i: runs command with empty environment
Practical examples of each command

questions.question: Explain the significance of PATH variable. How do you add a directory to PATH temporarily and permanently? Demonstrate with commands.
keyPoints: PATH defines where system looks for executables
Critical for running commands without specifying full path
Directories separated by colon (:)
Temporary: export PATH=$PATH:/new/dir (current session only)
Permanent: Add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.profile
Use echo $PATH to view current PATH
Order matters - first matching executable is used
Security implication: avoid including . in PATH

questions.question: Write and explain the difference between set, env, and printenv commands with suitable examples.
keyPoints: set: Shows all shell variables, environment variables, and shell functions
env: Shows only exported environment variables, can run commands in modified environment
printenv: Shows only environment variables, can display specific variable
Example set output includes function definitions
Example env output is cleaner, only exported vars
printenv HOME shows only HOME value
Use cases: set for debugging, env for running with modified env, printenv for specific vars
